questions.prompt: A firm's bonds yield 6.0% YTM. Tax rate is 25%. What is the after-tax cost of debt? Answer as a percent.
questions.explanation: After-tax r_D = 6.0 × (1 − 0.25) = 4.5%. WACC always uses this version because interest is tax-deductible.

questions.prompt: Why should you NOT use a legacy bond's coupon rate as the firm's cost of debt today?
choices: The coupon reflects credit conditions when the bond was issued; today's market YTM reflects what new debt would actually cost
Coupons are quoted in different currency
questions.explanation: A 2020 3% coupon bond now trading at 6% YTM has a market cost of debt of 6%, not 3%. Cost of capital is forward-looking.

questions.prompt: A consumer-staples firm (low WACC) is starting a biotech R&D division. What discount rate should it use to evaluate the biotech project?
choices: The firm's WACC, because that's the firm's cost of capital
A higher project-specific rate reflecting biotech's actual risk; use unlevered beta of pure-play biotech comparables, re-levered at the project's planned capital structure
The Treasury yield, because R&D is exploratory
Whatever rate makes the project look positive NPV
questions.explanation: Using firm WACC when project risk differs causes systematic over- or under-investment. Use a project-specific rate from pure-play comparables.

questions.prompt: Estimating beta for a PRIVATE firm without public comparables would typically follow which steps?
choices: Unlever each comparable's beta to isolate business risk from financial structure
Use the firm's own stock-price history (which doesn't exist)
Average the unlevered betas, then re-lever at the target's capital structure
questions.explanation: Comparables → unlever → average → re-lever is the standard recipe. A private firm has no public price history, so direct regression isn't possible.
